---
title: "Standards Context"
sidebar: intro
---

The proposal is not a new data-center exchange standard. It is a working model for local DASCore workflows that can borrow from standards without inheriting all of their required detail.

## StationXML

FDSN StationXML organizes conventional seismic metadata roughly as:

```text
Inventory -> Network -> Station -> Channel
```

DASCore keeps the useful top-level framing:

```text
Inventory -> Network -> FiberArray -> Acquisition
```

`FiberArray` is station-like because it represents a durable observing identity. It is not a point station; it is anchored by an extended optical path.

## FDSN DAS Metadata

The FDSN DAS Metadata draft separates concepts such as interrogator, data acquisition, cable, fiber, and channels. DASCore keeps these concepts but relocates them for working archive use:

| FDSN DAS concept | DASCore proposal |
|---|---|
| Interrogator | `Interrogator` resource or acquisition-linked object |
| Data acquisition | `Acquisition` under a `FiberArray` |
| Cable and fiber | `Cable`, `FiberSegment`, and other `OpticalComponent` items |
| Channels | Patch coordinates plus acquisition channel-to-distance metadata |
| Native headers | Controlled fields plus `extra_fields` where needed |

### Why Not Use FDSN DAS Metadata Directly?

The FDSN DAS Metadata work is the closest standards reference for DAS, and it is valuable because it gives the community shared names for interrogators, acquisitions, cables, fibers, and channel descriptions. The mismatch is that DASCore needs a working metadata model for analysis, not only an exchange record for a fully described data set.

Many field archives begin with incomplete or evolving information. A team may know the acquisition settings and a few tap-test locations, but not the complete cable construction, channel table, or final geometry. Later, the same archive may gain corrected distances, coupling notes, a revised CRS, or a new interpretation after a fiber break. DASCore needs those partial and revised states to be easy to represent without forcing users into a complete submission-grade schema.

FDSN-style channel descriptions can also become awkward for DAS because the channel count is often large and the channel identity is not always the most useful physical coordinate. In DASCore workflows, a patch may start with `channel`, `index`, or `distance`; the inventory can convert channel or index positions into optical distance when needed, and geometry or coupling can then be projected along that optical-distance axis.

The proposal therefore uses FDSN DAS Metadata as a terminology and interoperability reference, but keeps DASCore's storage shape smaller and more analysis-oriented. The intended path is not to reject FDSN, but to make a practical sidecar model that could later be exported to a richer FDSN-compatible record when the necessary metadata exists.

The main tradeoff is channel modeling. The proposal avoids storing large channel tables in the first schema. Instead, channel or index coordinates can be converted to optical distance using acquisition metadata when needed.

## PRODML

PRODML is especially useful for optical path vocabulary: fibers, connectors, splices, couplers, turnarounds, and terminations. DASCore adopts that path-centered thinking, but uses ordered interval sequences rather than a full connectivity network.

### Why Not Use PRODML Directly?

PRODML is a strong reference for oil-and-gas fiber installations, especially wells and production systems where the surrounding operational metadata already fits the Energistics ecosystem. Many DAS experiments do not look like that. They may use telecom dark fiber, buried civil-infrastructure cable, railway or road corridors, glacier deployments, geothermal surveys, lab coils, temporary trenched arrays, or improvised field installations.

For those archives, a PRODML-first record can be too specialized and too heavy. Users may not know the full installation network, OTDR history, well context, or asset-management details that PRODML can represent. They may only have a tap test, a cable route sketch, acquisition settings, coupling notes, and a few correction events. DASCore needs that partial metadata to remain valid and useful.

The proposal therefore treats PRODML as a vocabulary source, not as the storage shape. DASCore keeps the optical path idea, but reduces the first model to the pieces most DAS analysis needs: optical distance, path components, geometry, coupling, annotations, acquisition context, and revision history.

That simpler model supports the first DASCore workflows: project geometry onto a patch, label intervals, add coupling metadata, revise interpretations, and keep incomplete path sections explicit.
